diary:
Monday started like most do—black coffee at the kitchen table with yesterday's paper, then a quiet walk down to the waterfront while the city was still half-asleep. The water was calm. Back home to shower and head to the office in Wallingford, where I spent the morning buried in case files and contract reviews. Nothing urgent, just the steady work.

Brunch at Matt's in the Market was good. I sat at the counter, eggs and toast, watching the Pike Place crowd move through. Old habits—I still count exits, still notice camera angles. Can't turn that off after forty years wearing the badge.

But the real day was watching my granddaughter's soccer match at Volunteer Park. She played hard, moved well. Afterwards we got ice cream and she told me about every play like I hadn't been standing there the whole time. Those moments matter more than I used to think they would.

Ended with a long drive through the city, no real destination. Just thinking. Then dinner alone at Ray's in Ballard. Quiet night overall.
thought_process: I wasn't thinking much about the World Cup or the light rail closure—those things barely registered. The day felt like it was supposed to: routine work in the morning, then pivoting toward what actually matters. Watching my granddaughter play, that's where my attention needed to be. The office work is necessary, keeps me sharp, but it's become background noise. I took the long drive in the evening because sometimes you need to move through the city without a destination, just to clear your head and see what's changed. The observation habits never leave you—I notice things most people don't—but I'm learning to use them differently now. Less about solving cases, more about understanding how people move through their lives. That shift has been gradual, but today it felt clear.
world_event_prompt: Sunny Monday in early June. FIFA World Cup group stage match (USA vs Wales) airs at 11am PDT. Light Rail closed between Westlake and University District for maintenance until 3pm.
